nativeRange.habitat: Found across a range of shallow Indo-Pacific habitats including coral reefs, rubble zones, sandy bottoms, seagrass patches, and intertidal and subtidal flats, typically from 1 to 30 metres depth. In the wild it is most often seen carrying a gastropod shell onto which it has transplanted one or more sea anemones — most commonly Calliactis polypus — as a mutualistic defence against octopus predators.

decorPreferences: large amounts of stable, well-anchored live rock that will not topple when the crab climbs or excavates
open sand or rubble bottom for foraging
dim or subdued lighting; the species is generally more active at low light levels
a selection of empty gastropod shells in progressively larger sizes for the crab to upgrade into as it grows
compatible host anemones (e.g. Calliactis spp.) for the natural defensive partnership, if available

diet.notes: The Jeweled Anemone Hermit Crab is an opportunistic omnivore that generally accepts a wide range of prepared foods, including mysis shrimp, finely chopped raw seafood such as shrimp, scallop, clam, and mussel, silversides, sinking pellets, and dried marine algae or nori. It also grazes on filamentous algae, cyanobacteria, and detritus. At adult size it becomes predatory enough to take snails, ornamental shrimp, polychaete worms, and small slow-moving fish when the opportunity arises. Iodine and trace-element supplementation is commonly recommended to support healthy molting.

goodWith: robust fish that are too large or fast to be ambushed
tangs, larger wrasses and other peaceful but mobile reef fish that can hold their own
compatible Calliactis-type sea anemones intended as the crab's defensive partner
stable, well-aged FOWLR or invertebrate display systems
avoidWith: ornamental snails and other hermit crabs (will be killed for shells)
small ornamental shrimp (cleaner, peppermint, sexy shrimp)
delicate sessile invertebrates within reach (LPS frags, zoanthid colonies, clams) that can be knocked, climbed on or nipped
small slow-moving fish that may be ambushed at night (e.g. small gobies, dragonets, firefish at adult crab size)
octopus and large predatory fish (triggerfish, large pufferfish, large wrasses) that prey on hermits
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: The Jeweled Anemone Hermit Crab (Dardanus pedunculatus) is a large Indo-Pacific species known for actively recruiting sea anemones onto its shell as living armour. Generally considered hardy and suitable for beginners, though its adult-size aggression calls for careful tankmate selection.
careNotes:
The Jeweled Anemone Hermit Crab has a mottled tan-and-cream carapace, distinctive red-banded white eyestalks, and a markedly oversized left claw — a feature that places it in the family Diogenidae, the so-called left-handed hermit crabs. Its most recognisable behaviour is the active recruitment of sea anemones onto its shell. In the wild the crab most commonly uses Calliactis polypus for this purpose: it strokes and taps the anemone until it releases its grip, then transplants it onto its own shell, and repeats the transfer whenever it moves to a larger shell. The partnership provides defence against octopus predators. In captivity the symbiosis is interesting to observe but is not required for the crab's survival.

The species is generally considered hardy and suitable for beginners in terms of day-to-day care, but its adult-size aggression raises the practical difficulty of keeping it alongside other animals. Like most large Dardanus species, adults tend to kill ornamental snails, smaller hermit crabs, and shrimp — either to acquire their shells or as food — and will opportunistically target small slow-moving fish. The species is generally regarded as reef-safe with caution rather than fully reef-safe: it has been documented climbing on and dislodging coral frags, and occasionally nipping at LPS polyps (large-polyp stony corals), though outright coral consumption appears less common than with some other large hermit species.

Always provide a selection of empty gastropod shells in a range of sizes so the crab can upgrade as it grows; the absence of suitable shells is a commonly cited cause of aggression toward tank snails. As with all marine invertebrates, the Jeweled Anemone Hermit Crab is highly intolerant of copper-based medications and must not be kept in any system that has been recently treated with them.
breedingNotes: Sexes are separate. Females carry orange-pink egg masses on their pleopods (the small swimming appendages on the abdomen), which hatch into free-swimming zoea larvae. Larval rearing is widely regarded as impractical in standard reef aquariums because the larvae spend an extended period in the water column, are readily lost to filtration and protein skimming, and have demanding live-food requirements. All aquarium specimens are wild-collected.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied LiveAquaria root resolved to this product page via search. Provided baseline husbandry parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12), care level easy, aggressive temperament, omnivorous diet, copper intolerance and iodine/trace-element supplementation recommendation.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked common name, adult size (4 in ~ 10 cm), minimum tank size (24 US gal), beginner care level, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe with caution, Indo-Pacific range and the anemone-on-shell symbiosis. Disagreed with LiveAquaria on temperament (semi-aggressive vs aggressive) and reef-safety (with-caution vs no); averaged to semi-aggressive / with-caution.

additional.name: Reefguide.org (Florent's Guide to the Tropical Reefs)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Diogenidae, left-handed hermits), depth range (0-30 m), Indo-Pacific + Hawaii distribution, and a lower size estimate of 2-7 cm typical adult body (averaged with retailer 10 cm max into the recorded 5-10 cm).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Dardanus pedunculatus)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Diogenidae, Decapoda, Anomura), full Indo-Pacific range (Seychelles to Japan and Hawaii, including eastern Australia), depth range 1-27 m, maximum size 10 cm, asymmetric left-claw morphology, octopus-predator defence rationale for the anemone partnership, and the note that this species has not been successfully reared in captivity.

additional.name: Saltwaterfish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank size (40 US gal ~ 151 L; supported the bias-larger choice of 150 L for adult tank-mate aggression), temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.0-8.4, specific gravity 1.023-1.026, semi-aggressive temperament and reef-safe-with-caution rating. Outlier maximum size of 6 in (15 cm) was averaged out against the other sources.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Larger Dardanus hermit crabs molt far less frequently than small ornamental shrimp. Healthy adults are generally reported to molt roughly every one to three months, with juveniles molting more often as they grow. Before and after a molt, the crab typically retreats deep into its shell or into a sheltered crevice for one to two days, and it is highly vulnerable during this window. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium, iodine, and trace-element levels all support successful molts; sudden swings in water parameters are the most commonly cited cause of failed or incomplete molts. Shed exoskeletons can be left in the aquarium, as they provide a useful source of calcium and chitin for the crab to reabsorb.
crustacean.breedingNotes: The Jeweled Anemone Hermit Crab forms no lasting pair bond, and mating is brief. After mating, females brood the fertilised eggs on their pleopods (the small abdominal appendages used for swimming) until the eggs hatch into planktonic larvae. The larvae have not been successfully reared at any meaningful scale in home aquariums, as they require extended time in open water, are readily lost to filtration and protein skimming, and have demanding live-food requirements. All aquarium specimens are wild-collected from the Indo-Pacific.
